displayTitle: Stack the Factors: Field Exposure Math

beats.narration: Level Two owns the technique. Source decayed? Distance forced out? You adjust the numbers, because every retake costs film, schedule, and dose.

payload.title: STACK THE FACTORS
payload.sub: Re-figure the technique — right the first time.

beats.narration: Three corrections cover most field changes. Decay: inverse with activity. Distance: the square of the ratio. Density: a factor from the film curve.
One factor at a time, then multiply. Activity halved? Eight minutes becomes sixteen. Thirty to forty inches? Squared: times one point seven eight.

payload.heading: Worked example: decay, then distance
payload.note: ONE FACTOR AT A TIME, THEN MULTIPLY

beats.narration: Before computing time, check geometry. Codes cap unsharpness, so flip the formula, solve minimum distance, and keep film tight against the part.

payload.heading: The Ug cap sets your minimum distance
payload.note: d ≥ F·t ÷ Ug MAX — BEFORE THE TIME

beats.narration: So work the chain: one factor, multiply, write it down. Distance goes by the square, never linear. The next crew inherits arithmetic, not folklore.
